data.title: UiPath: Ignore The Bearish Noise And Buy This AI Gem
data.description:
Authored by Winfred Mwiti and Jacques Georget

Investment Thesis

Since our last article on UiPath Inc. (NYSE:PATH), the stock has lost ~20%. But as you are all aware, this is due to a broader market trend with the S&P500 falling by ~13% for the same period.

We believe that the future for PATH remains bright. The company is making strides to adapt to the booming AI market, including a strategic pivot towards Agentic AI. Furthermore, the company boasts strong customer retention signaling a solid product offering coupled with great customer satisfaction. There is also a large and rapidly growing TAM for PATH. In addition, our valuation model indicates that the stock is trading at a significant discount, offering a decent entry point.

The Broader Tech Sell-Off & The Risk It Poses To UiPath

Among the major headlines in the world today is the significant turbulence experienced by tech stocks in recent months. For instance, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) is in a strong downtrend, recently losing more than 11% YTD.

The ongoing volatility has been driven by both macroeconomic and geopolitical factors, most notably the escalating global trade wars sparked by President Trump’s aggressive tariff policies. To demonstrate how the ongoing trade wars have catalyzed the ongoing volatility, the DJI plunged by more than 2,200 points in a single day recently following China’s retaliation against U.S. tariffs, signaling heightened economic uncertainty. PATH hasn’t been immune to this turmoil, with the stock declining more than 21% YTD.

While these trade wars are likely to persist in the near future, leading to a potential recession, we find three major risks which it poses to PATH. Firstly, we see a potential for reduced enterprise spending and PATH’s customer base being primarily large enterprises which rely heavily on discretionary budgets for automation could see tighter budgets, perhaps stagnating its ARR.

Additionally, the 25% tax proposal on goods from Canada and Mexico could increase the cost of hardware and infrastructure that PATH clients depend on for deploying automation solutions. For example, higher data center costs are a concern already for Big Tech AI ambitions, something that could trickle down to the UiPath ecosystem, making its solutions less cost competitive.

Lastly, we anticipate weak market sentiments if the current geopolitical climate persists. With the market having turned bearish already, we expect investors to be increasingly wary of the current momentum and despite its strong fundamentals which we shall discuss shortly; the weak market sentiment could exacerbate downward pressure on its stocks at least in the short run.

Nevertheless, despite these potential risks, we believe the company’s AI innovations and its fundamentals (market leadership and high customer retention) offer us solace.

PATH Still Leads In A High Growth Market

PATH is a dominant player in the RPA market, being a leader in robotic process automation. The RPA market is seeing a robust demand as businesses strive to streamline operations while reducing costs. Grand View Research estimates that the global RPA market will grow by a CAGR of 43.9% between 2025 and 2030.

Given the company’s dominant market position, leading with a 35.8% market share, UiPath is well-positioned to ride this strong market growth. Its integration of AI into its platform through capabilities such as ML, NLP and intelligent document processing give the company a competitive edge. Its end-to-end automation platform combining UI automation and API integration further cements its competitive advantage.

In our view, despite the deceleration in quarterly revenue growth over the last four quarters, this company’s consistent revenue growth, up 135% compared to 5-years ago, and its consistent ARR growth underscores the company’s ability to deliver irrespective of economic uncertainties.

Innovation: Pivoting To Agentic AI

One of the most compelling reasons we have for investing in PATH is its aggressive push into Agentic AI (the next-generation approach where AI agents autonomously perform complex tasks collaborating with humans and other systems). The company has introduced AI-powered products such as autopilot, agent builder, Agentic testing and Agentic orchestration. This evolution is positioning the company to compete in the rapidly growing AI market, which we expect to complement rather than replace its core RPA business model.

To demonstrate the impact of this shift, following the company’s launch of its Agent Builder Software in Dec 2024, it has garnered significant interest with more than 1,000 organizations signing up for private preview.

It is our view that this move addresses the concerns that generative AI could disrupt traditional RPA. With the quick numbers in the Agent Builder Software signaling the potential success of this new venture, it could reignite UiPath's growth trajectory as businesses increase adoption to AI-driven automation.

Why The Innovations Matter

In our view, we think that this company’s innovations offer a significant counterweight to the current challenges by positioning the company to weather the storms. To support this, we highlight two major aspects. To begin with, it will enhance its automation capabilities. Its recent innovation such as the Test cloud which leverages AI to streamline software testing, and the integration of the Agent Builder and Autopilot empowers enterprises to automate complex workflows such as those in SAP and Oracle environments. This addresses a critical point for businesses (faster innovation cycles) potentially making its offerings indispensable even during downturns.

Secondly, PATH has carved out a niche in enterprise automation through its recent innovations, potentially improving its growth potential. It is estimated that 90% of IT executives have processes that need Agentic AI to improve, with 77% being ready to invest in Agentic AI this year. This presents a tremendous growth opportunity for this company.

Customer Retention & Expansion

When we look at PATH’s customer metrics, we can see that its growth potential is anchored on a growing customer base. In its 2025 fiscal year, the company maintained a dollar-based net retention rate of 110%, an indication that its existing clients are not only staying but also spending more. Further, its number of high-value clients was on the rise. Its customers with $100,000 or more in ARR grew to 2,292 in Q4 2025 up from 2,054 in Q4 2024, with those with $1 million or more growing to 317 in Q4 2025 from 288 the previous year.

Above all, in the 2025 fiscal year, customers with $5 million or more in ARR saw a 30% growth. With this expansion and a total customer base of about 10,750, we think the company’s ability to deepen relationships with customers and grow its customer base has been demonstrated strongly.

Financial Projection & Valuation

Our financial projections for PATH assume a revenue growth rate of 6.8% in 2026 and 9% in 2027 respectively. The low growth rate for 2026 is reflective of the cautious outlook that management has for the year. It also reflects the recent decline in YoY revenue growth.

However, we have a more optimistic view for 2027 as we expect that the company's recent products will have started impacting its top line, while restoring its upward growth trajectory. We also expect improvements in PATH's EBITDA margin, driven by the company’s shift to its high-margin AI-driven product offerings.

In light of the above projections, we value this stock using the P/S valuation method while comparing PATH to some of its peers. From our comparative analysis, we find that PATH is trading at a significant discount. The peer group's average forward PS ratio is 7.68x, while PATH’s forward PS multiple sits at only 3.62. This disparity is not justified, particularly considering that PATH's 3-year revenue CAGR of ~17% is just a few percentage points below that of the peer group.

As such, we value this stock using a PS multiple of 7x, which is slightly below the peer group average, reflecting the upside potential from its share buybacks and growth initiatives such as the shift to Agentic AI. Assuming this multiple, we estimate a target price of ~$21 per share.

Share Buybacks to Boast the Stock Price

In FY 2025, PATH authorized $500 million for share buybacks, bringing the total to $554 million. Assuming its current stock price of ~$10, this program could repurchase approximately 49.8 million shares of the 551.49 million outstanding shares, approximately 9% of the outstanding shares. This could boost EPS by ~9%, making the stock more attractive to investors and supporting a higher valuation.

Looking at the balance sheet, this company has a strong liquidity given its cash balance of $1.63 billion. It then follows that the $500 million shares repurchase approval would leave the company with about $1.1 billion in cash reserve, which is ample liquidity to support both development and potentially expanding further on its share buyback program.

Risks

As with all investments, PATH has its risks as well. The shift towards generative AI raises questions as to whether RPA could be fully commoditized. Nevertheless, the company’s integration of AI into its platforms mitigates this threat and positions it as a hybrid player that stands between traditional automation and next-generation AI solutions. Economic uncertainty is another risk that we are seeing given the slowing global economic growth. The negative effect could be seen in the weak guidance for Q1 FY 2026.

Takeaway Message

PATH's fundamentals tell a very tempting story. The company is a market leader in RPA and can boast of a large and growing customer base. Furthermore, its efforts towards AI integration seems like the perfect bet and will help the company capture a major portion of its large and rapidly growing TAM. PATH also recently expanded on its share buyback program, which should give another push to the stock price. All in all, this translates into a unique buying opportunity. Considering the sharp correction in the stock price, this company is poised for a comeback.

data.title: Tilray: Sell Before Potential Reverse Split
data.description:
One of the worst performing names in the market continues to be Tilray (NASDAQ:TLRY). The Canadian cannabis and beverage firm has seen its shares plunge more than 92% over the past five years as revenue growth has not materialized as expected and significant cash burn has led to substantial dilution. On Tuesday, the company reported its fiscal third quarter results, which were not very good. Despite shares hitting a new multi-year low after the report, there still could be plenty of downside ahead.

Previous Coverage Of The Name

I last looked at Tilray back in mid-March, at which point short interest in the name had surged to a new high. As shares plunged and the company was swapping debt for equity, the number of shares short continued to rise. It didn't help that analysts were continuing to reduce their price targets, and fears over a US / Canada trade war were hurting overall economic sentiment.

Since I went to a sell rating on Tilray back in January, shares have lost about 57% of their value. US markets have declined a little over that time, but certainly not to that degree. I've warned recently that Tilray would likely need to reverse split its stock to maintain exchange listing requirements, and the chances of that have only increased since, with shares continuing to trade well below the $1.00 threshold they need to be at.

The Fiscal Q3 Report

Tilray's numbers are a bit messy at the moment as it looks to reorganize the business. Net revenues for Q3 came in at less than $186 million, or $193 million in constant currency. The net number was down a few million from the prior year period, a quarter that contained about $6 million of revenue from now discontinued items. Management noted that strategic initiatives and SKU rationalization impacted total revenue generated by $13.2 million in the current year quarter.

No matter which way you slice it, the number was a major disappointment, as the street was looking for almost $210 million for the period. Beverage revenue was up less than 3% year over year, hit hard by a $6 million impact from the SKU rationalization. Net cannabis revenues were down more than 14% year over year, impacted by currencies, the timing of moving certain product to international markets, and the decision to pause the company's presence in certain items such as vapes and infused pre-rolls.

The company did report a two percentage point increase in adjusted beverage margins, but adjusted cannabis margins fell by eight percentage points. This led to an overall one percentage point decrease in company-wide adjusted gross margins. The company swung to an adjusted loss of $2.9 million in the period from a $0.9 million profit a year earlier. However, this essentially resulted in a break-even loss per share, three cents better than the street was looking for. On a GAAP basis, the company lost nearly $800 million, mostly thanks to a roughly $700 million impairment charge.

Between currency issues and the SKU rationalization efforts, Tilray reduced its fiscal year revenue guidance by $50 million. The company is now looking for $850 million to $900 million. I noted previously how analysts were skeptical of the previously higher range, and that stance was partially reflected in the cut here today, but the new guidance midpoint is still well below the $892 million analyst average estimate that was seen entering Tuesday.

More Dilution Coming

One of the main issues I've had with Tilray over the years is its financial situation. Large losses have led to major cash burn and dilution. The company burned roughly $20 million in Q3, although this was an improvement of about $4 million over the prior year period. However, if we look at the company's adjusted free cash flow metric, the more than $18 million burn was a stark reversal from the $0.6 million generated in fiscal Q3 2024.

Tilray ended the February 2025 period with about $248 million in cash and marketable securities, and a little more than $277 million in total debt. The net debt number there did improve by about $28.7 million in the period, partially thanks to the debt for equity swaps I mentioned in my prior article. However, that meant that investors saw another round of dilution, with the share count hitting another new high as seen in the chart below.

At more than 983 million at the end of February, the number of shares outstanding here has basically doubled in three years. Back in December, the company received shareholder approval to increase its authorized share count from 1.208 billion to 1.426 billion. As a reminder, the company is not only swapping debt for equity at this time, but using an outstanding equity sales program to raise cash in most quarters. The outstanding share count will likely top 1 billion at some point this year, although the physical number could change dramatically if we do get a reverse split.

A Look At The Valuation Picture

On the valuation front, Tilray finished trading Thursday for about 0.56 times the current revenue expected for the May 2025 fiscal year. That's a meaningful discount to the valuation that peer Canopy Growth (CGC) went for at 0.69 times its March 2025 expected revenue. Tilray is projected to see more revenue growth in its current fiscal year, but Canopy is expected to show a little more when each gets to its next respective fiscal period. The gap in valuation between these two has narrowed quite a bit since I last looked at these two companies' price to sales numbers, but both have come down.

Perhaps surprisingly, street analysts are still extremely positive on Tilray. The average price target as seen in the graphic below is currently $1.33, which would imply more than 160% upside from current levels. I should note, however, that the street average was $2.60 a year ago and nearly $10 just three years ago, so analysts have continued to cut their targets over time and seemingly are just playing a game of catch up as the stock drops further.

My main negative catalyst to watch in the near term continues to be a potential reverse split, with the stock well under a dollar per share. I've covered numerous reverse splits over the years on this site, and usually, we get a leg down on the announcement and another once the reverse split actually happens. Barring some dramatic rally in shares, the only question now appears to be what the reverse split ratio will be.

The only way I could see shares surging in the near term is if this company somehow gets bought out. Perhaps a larger player in the beverage space thinks there is enough synergies from a merger to make this business work, or some private equity firm thinks it can reduce debt here and cut enough costs out over time. The only thing that makes this a little hard to see right now is that Tilray bought a lot of its beverage brands from larger firms that didn't seem to want them, and the cannabis business continues to struggle in the near term.

Final Thoughts And Recommendation

In the end, Tilray announced another set of disappointing results on Tuesday. Even excluding headwinds from some discontinued products, revenues came in well short of street estimates. While the adjusted loss wasn't as bad as expected, the company is still burning cash at a decent clip. The net debt balance only improved because investors were diluted meaningfully thanks to share sales and debt for equity swaps.

With a reverse split increasingly likely, I have to maintain my sell rating on the stock today. The company continues to disappoint when it comes to revenue growth, and it hasn't been able to deliver any sustained positive cash flow over time. For me to increase my rating moving forward, those two items have to show some decent improvement, and we have to see dilution show some signs that it will be leveling off. Even though shares have been hit hard in recent months, more new lows seem possible here.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMOM: The Experiment Has Failed For This AI-Powered Momentum ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The QRAFT AI-Enhanced U.S. Large Cap Momentum ETF (NYSEARCA:AMOM) has been a huge disappointment since its launch six years ago, and with a high 0.75% expense ratio and just $23 million in assets under management, I don't expect this ETF to survive much longer. In addition to reviewing its performance and strongly urging shareholders to sell AMOM now, I'll highlight some historical and current fundamentals that help explain why AMOM has struggled, which I hope you can use to identify a more suitable momentum fund for the long run. I hope you enjoy the read.

AMOM Overview

Strategy Discussion

AMOM's strategy is proprietary, so we don't know exactly how QRAFT's AI is designed. However, an explanatory video and a general overview of its process on its website note the following:

1. The AI utilizes machine learning to explore "a vast database to narrow down valid momentum factor stocks" using macro, price, and fundamental data. This "Data Pre-Processing" phase aims to remove survivorship bias and look ahead bias and helps ensure the AI model "is accurate and in the right time frame."

2. The AI performs scenario analysis to test various factor combinations. In this "Factor Factory" phase, the AI aims to discover investment opportunities related to a company's quality, size, value, momentum, and low volatility. As a momentum ETF, AMOM focuses on the momentum factor, though it's unclear how many other factors count.

3. The AI constructs a portfolio of the top 50 stocks it believes can deliver stronger returns than conventional momentum investing strategies. In this "Strategy Factory" phase, the data is back- and forward-tested with training and test data.

4. The portfolio rebalances monthly to ensure consistent exposure to high-momentum stocks.

The last point is no joke, either. Not only does the portfolio rebalance monthly, but it essentially substitutes about half the stocks each time. As shown below, AMOM's portfolio turnover rate ranged between 346% and 790% between 2021 and 2024. Taking the average annual rate of 523%, that means roughly 45% of the portfolio is swapped each month on average.

With these high-turnover funds, it is crucial shareholders don't get tied to the actual holdings. Instead, they should focus on factor exposures instead. I mentioned in point #2 how it was unclear how well AMOM emphasized other non-momentum factors, but fortunately, I've kept pretty good records over the last year, and I should be able to shed some light on that unknown.

Performance vs. Peer Group

As I mentioned earlier, AMOM has a costly 0.75% expense ratio, and it's probably one reason for its low $23 million in assets under management. At this level, QRAFT is only bringing in about $175K in fees each year, which is hardly enough to support an ETF. That is why I believe it's a going concern, especially since it's almost six years old and there are many other alternatives available, including the four listed below.

  1. Invesco S&P 500® Momentum ETF (SPMO).
  2. iShares MSCI USA Momentum Factor ETF (MTUM).
  3. JPMorgan U.S. Momentum Factor ETF (JMOM).
  4. SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Momentum Factor ETF (SEIM).

Not only are these ETFs cheaper and more popular, but they've also delivered superior returns. I've excluded SEIM from the performance chart below due to its later launch date, but AMOM has lagged behind SPMO and JMOM since its inception on May 20, 2019.

It still has the edge over MTUM (105.46% vs. 89.64%), but it's remarkable how poorly it's performed since November 2021. As highlighted, its $19,884 growth on a $10K investment translated into a 99% gain to the end of October 2021, which means it's only added 6% in the last 3+ years. These results cast doubt that its AI is better than the conventional momentum strategies it seeks to outperform.

AMOM Fundamentals

Factor Ranks Since July 2024

Beginning in July 2024, I started compiling start-of-month factor rankings for nearly 1,000 U.S. Equity ETFs. These rankings provide a snapshot of the factors funds emphasize each month, which is important for high-turnover proprietary funds like AMOM. Take a look over the table below, and please note that a lower rank indicates AMOM had higher exposure to the factor.

Here are three quick takeaways:

1. AMOM consistently ranks well on the momentum factor, which confirms the two systems generally align. That's what we want to see from any momentum fund, and in the last 11 months, AMOM has also consistently scored well on the growth factor and low on the risk and size factors. That's normal. AMOM is a large-cap ETF, so I don't expect it to hold smaller companies, and higher volatility usually comes with the territory when investing in growth stocks.

2. Momentum and sentiment (earnings revisions, sales/earnings surprises) are normally highly correlated because stocks with strong price momentum usually have catalysts like good earnings reports or earnings upgrades. However, AMOM's sentiment rank has bounced around, which I captured in the "standard deviation" column on the right. Since AI doesn't seem to factor in these statistics, this increases the chance that shareholders could end up holding high-momentum stocks even after they've reported poor results or had their earnings outlook downgraded by Wall Street analysts.

3. AMOM's quality rank is inconsistent, ranging between #88 in November 2024 to #393 in March 2025. The fund's quality started to decline in January 2025 and remained poor up until the start of April, though as we'll see shortly, that's reversed again after this month's rebalancing. Still, the damage has been done. Since AMOM's quality rank dipped in January 2025, it's lagged behind SPMO by 12.86% YTD through April 9.

Current Fundamental Analysis: Top 25 Holdings

With these factors in mind, let's now look at how AMOM compares with SPMO, MTUM, JMOM, and SEIM fundamentally. The following table highlights selected statistics for its top 25 holdings, which comprise 73.95% of the portfolio.

This portfolio actually looks quite solid. AMOM's weighted average of 27.67% and 13.01% ROE and ROA figures are terrific and feed into its 9.39/10 sector-adjusted profit score, which I derived from Seeking Alpha Factor Grades. I don't see any major sentiment concerns, as its 7.01/10 EPS Revision Score ranks #2 behind SEIM's. It trades at a pretty expensive 24.79x forward earnings after Wednesday's historic gains, but it compensates investors with its 24.61% one-year estimated earnings growth rate, the best in the peer group. Lastly, AMOM's 1.15 five-year portfolio beta suggests that it might even be less risky than SPMO, even with 9.57% allocated to NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

However, readers must remember this is only a snapshot, and if history is any guide, these holdings will change completely in a matter of months. My main concern is a decline in quality led by stocks like MicroStrategy Incorporated (MSTR) and Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD), which AMOM held just two months ago. If QRAFT could tighten this up and not allow such speculative companies into the fund, I believe results would improve.

Investment Recommendation

AMOM is an AI-powered large-cap momentum fund aiming to outperform conventional momentum ETFs like SPMO, MTUM, JMOM, and SEIM. Its 0.75% expense ratio is one drawback, but in my judgment, its main flaw is a lack of consistency emphasizing non-momentum factors like quality. Monthly portfolio turnover approaches 50% per month on average, and although AMOM looks fundamentally strong today, I have no confidence that will be the case moving forward. As a result, AMOM is a single-factor fund that's destined to deliver inconsistent returns, as illustrated by Morningstar's quartile and percentile rankings below.

Even more concerning is AMOM's $23 million in AUM amassed over the last six years. Given the costs of running an ETF, I doubt AMOM is profitable, so I wouldn't be surprised to see it liquidate soon. Therefore, I recommend owners "sell" AMOM today and consider one of the four alternatives presented today. JMOM and SEIM rank high on my end, but if you'd like more information on another momentum fund not mentioned today, please feel free to ask in the comments section below. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Baidu: A Bet For Chinese AI
data.description:
Overview

Baidu, Inc. (NASDAQ:BIDU) is a collection of businesses that can be compared to Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL). Its core business is digital marketing, with a focus on its Chinese search engine. As Alphabet, Baidu is a prominent supplier of cloud services, online services, and AI model development, transforming all of its services and developing products to compete with DeepSeek, OpenAI, or Google’s Gemini. Baidu operates a highly advanced robotaxi business that has a limited impact on its financials.

Looking at Figure 1, one could conclude that the current valuation, measured as Price-To-Cash Flow, at x10 is cheap regarding Alphabet, which is x14.7, but at a historical x20. Baidu has reached a value of x30 in 2021 and 2017. So you could think that the company can extend its multiple.

Macro conditions in China have penalized Baidu’s growth, and the company has made its core business more efficient. However, higher costs in its streaming business have lagged profitability, which has pushed down its valuation to 10 times Price-To-Cash. This is an opportunity to invest in a leading Chinese AI company. Even though the company faces tough challenges, I think it is worth investing in one of the most prominent Chinese companies with a valuation of $107 per share, a 30% premium over its current stock price.

A collection of businesses with challenging conditions

Baidu's revenue decreased by 1% to RMB133 billion. The main segment, Baidu Core, grows minimally at 1% to RMB 104 billion in revenue, driven by a combination of stagnant Online Marketing Services due to the Chinese economic slowdown and an exploding AI Cloud business.

Online marketing services decreased to RMB 73.0 billion, a 3% decrease. Baidu is losing unexpectedly against Microsoft’s Bing (Figure 2). Baidu lost 15 percentage points of market share from 68.5%, especially in Desktop searches. Tencent is another potential thread as it is upgrading search capabilities in WeChat, with approximately 840 million daily users, and where it is getting 80% of search results in AI-generated content versus 20% in the case of Baidu. Besides Tencent, has integrated search functionality between Sogou and its WeChat.

AI Cloud services’ revenue in 2024 was RMB 21.9 billion, growing at 17%, accelerating in the last quarter with a growth rate of 26% year over year. However, Alphabet is increasing its Cloud business at a faster pace, +30.6% year over year in 2024, in a much bigger operation: $43.2 billion vs $3.0 billion in the case of Baidu in dollar equivalent value. However, it beats Alibaba Group Holding Limited's (BABA) yearly growth rate of 13% in Q4 2024.

Baidu’s Large Language Model, ERNIE, managed 1.65 billion API calls at the end of 2024, and the monthly average number of users of Wenku AI features was 94 million, constituting a driving force of its core business.

The segment Others inside Baidu Core is where its intelligent driving services, the robotaxi business, are held. This segment hardly grows by 3% to a total of RMB 9.9 billion. The robotaxi business has accumulated more than 130 million autonomous kilometers, expanding its service to fully driverless operations across the country. This year, the company will be able to expand its service to Hong Kong, a city with a population of 7.5 million and one of the most densely populated cities in the world. However, its aggressive pricing has resulted in a minimal impact on the income statement.

iQIYI, the streaming platform of the company, is down 8% to RMB 29.2 billion. In this segment, the company has problems, not only with growth but with costs. The company is shifting its focus to higher content quality and taking advantage of AI capabilities, but customer retention is tough in a highly competitive market.

Better efficiency

Operating income improved in 2023 from 12.9% to 16.2% but decreased to 15.5% in 2024 due to the higher content production cost of iQIYI. As you can see in Figure 3, the iQIYI operating income ratio has decreased from 9.4% in 2023 to 6.2% in 2024. This segment drags overall results because Baidu Core is in good shape with respect to operating margin increasing to 18.6% in 2024 from 18.2% a year ago. This increase in margins is done even when traffic acquisition costs have increased in the last year.

In Figure 4, you can see how Baidu is scaling the business. Research and development costs have decreased from 18.9% in 2022 to 16.6% in 2024. The cost of revenue decreased from 51.7% to 49.7% during the same period, despite a challenging environment for investment in cloud and traffic acquisition. We can observe the efforts the company has to make in promotional campaigns to counter declining digital ad revenue or cloud and AI services communications. Selling and administrative costs have increased from 16.6% of revenue in 2022 to 17.7% in 2024.

Overall, the company is making a rewarding effort to be more efficient, which I value.

An AI company

AI is at the core of Baidu's business. AI is the growth engine of the company; it is transforming the search service, it is the intelligence of its robotaxi business, and the efficiency of its streaming and cloud business. ERNIE, its generative large language model, has evolved to get what the company announced as similar to a GPT4-type model. Its developer community has grown to 19 million in 2024, handling 1.65 billion API calls daily. The company is delivering the model as open source, following similar trends in the market as Meta and DeepSeek. The reasoning behind this model is that the inference business will be more lucrative as it is bigger than training, and as adoption among the developer community is bigger, its revenue will increase. So far, the impact on the income statement is the revenue growth of 26% of its AI Cloud in Q4 2024.

CAPEX investment in Baidu has been RMB 6.5 billion in 2024, much lower than in 2023 at RMB 11.2 billion. The proportion with respect to revenue has almost halved to 4.8% from 8.3%. Considering that CAPEX is spent on servers, network equipment, and other computer hardware for AI Cloud infrastructure, this decrease does not fit with the concept of AI expansion. For example, Alphabet has invested 15% of its revenue in Cloud infrastructure, up from 10.5% in 2023, which means Google is increasing its investment and is almost double what Baidu spends. However, in the Chinese context, Alibaba spent RMB 23.2 billion in 2024, which was half of the RMB 55.2 billion in 2023, which is similar to Baidu's evolution but on a larger scale. However, Tencent has raised its CAPEX in 2024 more than three-fold to $10.7 billion stepping on the gas to be a leader in AI in China.

For Baidu, to be successful and stay competitive, AI is vital, but at risk of cannibalizing its core business. I think it is in a good position to get it.

Valuation

Figure 5 shows the company's value drivers, considering a year as the last four quarters to capture the latest information. Regarding margins, I utilize a measure I call Cash Margin, which involves adjusting net income for non-cash items such as amortization and depreciation, stock-based compensation, and deferred income tax.

I foresee two scenarios for the future of the company: a positive one and a negative one. I will describe both, and I will give a valuation of both scenarios. In both scenarios, I assume cash flows will be discounted at a 7.6% WACC because the beta is 1.11. The risk-free rate is 4.5%. The company's leverage is low at 26%, considering equity as market capitalization. The perpetual growth rate is set at 3%.

In the positive scenario, I assume that revenue will grow 1% in 2025, 5% in 2026, in line with analysts, and 8% in 2027. Later on, the growth rate will be sustained at 10% until 20234. I don’t expect explosive growth, but it will keep rising. The cash margin will be the same as the one in 2024, 16% for the entire period. This is a conservative assumption because the company has reached almost 38% in the last four years. CAPEX investment will be steady at 5% of revenue without the need to accelerate AI investments, which is an aggressive hypothesis due to the highly competitive AI market.

Based on Figure 6, I estimate a value of $153.4 per share, an 86% premium over its stock price. My valuation's implied Price to Free Cash Flow of 28.5 is close to the 30 level I talked about at the beginning of the article, which the company reached in 2017 and 2021.

In the negative scenario, I assume that the company just grows at 5% over the ten-period in my model because as the company expands its AI offering, it is cannibalizing its core search business, and the net effect is this 5% growth. This cannibalization is the main risk I foresee because I see it is hard to manage the transition. The other important risk is that, at a macro level, China cannot restore growth in its economy. The cash margin is the same 16% as the previous scenario, as I have stated, which is already conservative. The other important difference is that AI investment is 8% of revenue in the coming four years due to the highly competitive AI environment.

Based on Figure 7, I estimate a value of $61.0 per share, a 26% discount over its stock price. My valuation's implied Price to Free Cash Flow of 11.35 is what the company had in the last year, which was highly impacted by a slowdown in consumption and growth in China.

As I don’t have more information on which scenario is more likely to occur, I assign the same probability. So in Figure 8, you can see that the expected value for the company is $107.2 per share, a 30% premium over its current stock price.

Conclusion

I think China is a good place to invest in. The country has suffered an enormous real estate and debt crisis, and the government has established good conditions because it is going to support its companies to reestablish growth rates. Another interest for the Chinese government is to compete with its counterparts in the US in the strategic field of AI. Baidu is a good target in this AI space because. With its model ERNIE, it is one of the most advanced Chinese companies in AI Cloud, offering a lot of options in several businesses, like its advanced robotaxi service.

The company poses relevant risks, with the most significant being the potential cannibalization of its core search service by its AI technology; however, even with this and some battles lost against Microsoft's Bing, I recommend buying the stock at $107 per share with a 30% premium over its current stock price.

data.title: Coinbase: Minting Higher-Than-Expected Profits
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

The first closer look I've taken at Coinbase Global, Inc. (NASDAQ:COIN) was in August 2024, prior to being a Seeking Alpha Analyst. After my personal analysis, I initiated my first position the following September, during extreme fear in both the traditional stock market and crypto markets. Since then, the stock has come down over 50% due to a sharp reset in cryptocurrency prices and a broader S&P 500 bear market.

Finally, I now view Coinbase as a highly attractive buying opportunity again, with peak levels of fear in the market, analysts significantly underestimating near-term EPS, and being in a great financial position.

Coinbase's EPS estimates, in my view, will have to undergo a lot of revisions in the following years. EPS was $9.48 in 2024, so for 2025, a decline of 20% is anticipated. That's why FWD P/E is currently higher than its TTM P/E, but I'll go over valuation more detailed later.

Quarterly EPS estimates for 2025, which are those relevant for this article, show little fluctuation, ranging from around $1.80 to 2.10. Truthfully, I believe that analysts are going to be off by a lot here. Firstly, I think we will see higher EPS in 2025 than in 2024, but I also believe that quarters will show much greater variation than depicted below, which becomes more likely as Coinbase has historically both missed estimates by 85% and beat them by over 4000%.

Specifically, I believe Q1 will yield much lower EPS than anticipated due to significant losses in COIN's cryptocurrency holdings and lower transaction volume, both due to a sharp decline in the crypto market. Conversely, EPS will be much higher than anticipated in Q2 and Q4, possibly even in Q3. In the following, I'll highlight key reasons for these assumptions.

Why Do I Think So?

Recent Financials

Coinbase's Q4 2024 shareholder letter offers insights into a lot of compelling financial developments from last year. Here are the ones I think are particularly interesting:

  • Transaction revenue grew 162% YOY
  • EPS rose 2000%+ YOY, from $0.37 to $9.48
  • Conversely, operating expenses have grown by only 30%
  • They have net cash (cash + equivalents - long-term debt) of $5 billion, and total liquidity of $9.8 billion (including crypto investments)
  • Strong subscription growth of 64% YOY, reaching $2.3 billion, proving that diversification of revenue is progressing

Crypto Market

From a completely rational point of view, a crypto bull market continuation seems likely. Cycle theory, which is common amongst the crypto community, suggests Bitcoin will peak in Q4 2025 and only enter a bear market in 2026. As depicted below, we have not reached euphoric levels just yet.

According to analysts, Bitcoin has the potential to reach $160k this year, which would drive both explosive trading volume growth & cryptocurrency holdings gains for Coinbase.

A very bullish development for Coinbase is the accelerating adoption of institutional investors. Coinbase is the custodian for most major crypto ETFs that were launched in the last two years. It will likely continue to do so, with many more ETFs being scheduled to launch in 2025. Institutional assets under custody from ETFs alone reached $93 billion in 2024. While institutional transaction revenues are at $346 million, not representing a large share of revenue, their growth is immaculate, sitting at around 156% YOY. This growth is unlikely to abruptly come to a stop.

The seasonality of Bitcoin is another point that should be mentioned, October historically being Bitcoin's strongest month with an average return of 19.4%. This makes a Q4 BTC rally much more likely.

Regulatory Environment

Under the current administration, the regulatory environment around cryptocurrency trading and storing is set to change positively forever. Recently, some news emerged, showing that the SEC Case for Coinbase has been dropped, which has been confirmed from both parties and decreases COIN's risk of having to pay any fines or not being able to offer specific services anymore.

Technical Analysis

Technically, COIN stock is very oversold, sitting at a weekly RSI of 30, levels last seen in the 2022 bear market / tech stock crash. Coinbase's previous correction size averages at around -40%. We're now at approximately -50%, indicating a potential point of turnaround.

Additionally, we are now at an even lower point of fear than in August-September of 2024, during the brief yen-carry trade-led correction, both in the CNN Fear & Greed index, as well as in the Alternative Me Crypto Fear & Greed index.

Valuation

Coinbase's valuation has come down to fairly attractive levels. This can be seen when looking at its P/E and P/S ratio, which are approximately 18 and 7, respectively.

The real P/E ratio of Coinbase, however, is much lower due to its net cash position. After accounting for which, we arrive at a P/E and Forward P/E of 13.3 and 18.5, respectively, indicating that the market currently prices in a decline in earnings. Therefore, currently, being a cyclical play, Coinbase is being valued as if it was at its cyclical top. Since I do not believe the latter is the case, the stock is fundamentally undervalued to me at this price.

Compared to Robinhood (HOOD) (since Binance is not publicly traded), as I uncovered in my last article, which are trading at much richer valuations, namely a real (adjusted for a one-time tax benefit) P/E of 34, a Forward P/E of 28.3 and a P/S of 11. In comparison, Coinbase looks chiefly undervalued, especially since both of these companies have forward-looking estimates of shrinking earnings.

Due to the aforementioned aspects, I expect COIN's 2025 EPS to be higher than currently estimated. Let's get to an actual figure and return potential in the following.

Assumptions:

  • Crypto markets will reach their ATHs sometime this year.
  • Coinbase's crypto holdings will appreciate by around 50% until then.
  • Trading volume will hit $1.8 trillion, up 50% YOY.
  • Subscription revenues grow by 10% YOY.
  • Operating expenses increase 17% YOY, using scaling effects.

In this case, transaction revenues would reach $6 billion, which in addition to subscription revenue of $2.5 billion comes out to a total of $8.5 billion. Subtracting $5 billion of operating expenses and adding $1.2 billion of cryptocurrency investment gains, We arrive at $5.3 billion of pre-tax income. With an effective tax rate of 14%, net income would reach $4.56 billion. Dividing this by 267 million outstanding shares and 2025 EPS could reach $15.14. This would imply an earnings beat of 100%, justifying a higher P/E ratio of 22, which, multiplied with our EPS projection equals $333 as a share price, thus, implying a potential return of 87%.

Risks

Coinbase's profits are highly cyclical. While they have made some solid progress in diversifying their revenue mix, with most of their revenue still coming from transaction fees, they're highly dependent on economic strength and euphoric markets, which is not the sentiment of most investors as of recently.

The most obvious and simultaneously most detrimental risk to COIN's performance is a potential crypto bear market that could happen sooner than anticipated. Even worse, should tariffs cause a full-blown recession, consumer spending and stock market and crypto speculation would deteriorate as people need more money to spend on basic consumer goods. Combining this with COIN's beta of 2.6, and we would be in for a wild ride downwards, potentially similar to 2022, where the company lost 85% of its value.

The EU has announced a proposal of taxing U.S. technology companies as a reciprocal tariff countermeasure. Coinbase could be subject to these sanctions, but I personally believe this is unlikely to play out in the first place. The EU mentioned some company names, and Coinbase was not among them.

Another risk worth mentioning is increasing competition. Coinbase's main competitors are Robinhood and Binance, which is not publicly traded. Currently, the take rates of Coinbase, which represent the transaction fees, are very high compared to Robinhood, which might become more aligned in the future, leading to slowing transaction revenue growth for Coinbase.

There could also be new competitors emerging in the market due to the regulatory environment improving, which again might have a negative effect on margins.

Conclusion

Coinbase is a strong but cyclical business - perfect to acquire in a downturn like right now. Despite being almost 100% dependent on crypto markets, and bearing recession and competition risks, its EPS estimates for FY 2025 are too low and a near- to mid-term re-rating favoring long-directed investors is likely. Use the current extreme fear to position yourself. Strong buy.

Disclaimer: This is a rather high-risk, short-term investment play. I'm looking to hold COIN shares for anywhere between 2–8 months, but that might vary. I will follow this company more closely and post updates when I deem it relevant. I will be taking profits on the way up.

data.title: Park Hotels & Resorts: Unlikely To Achieve 2025 Financial Goals
data.description:
I rate Park Hotels & Resorts Inc. (NYSE:PK) a Hold, for income-focused investors interested in real estate investment trusts (REITs). While the dividend yield is very attractive, the risks of owning this REIT may not be worth it long term. The company’s history is volatile, subject to economic cycles and consumer sentiment toward travel. The dividend distribution is in a state of disorder, creating a gambling “Las Vegas like” mentality forcing the investor to ask – will I get lucky? Finally, I expect overall travel to slow down as the U.S. engages in a tariff war, increasing the costs of travel combined with negative sentiment toward the U.S. as a country as a destination.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the REIT are

  • Currently pays 13.9% dividend yield.
  • Large portfolio of premier hotel properties with luxury positioning
  • Inconsistent distribution payout strategy
  • Returned $3.8 billion of capital to shareholders in the form of stock repurchases or dividends.
  • Pipeline of development projects for growth
  • Travel both domestic and international will be stifled by increasing costs due to the tariff war.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the stock a Hold (as of 4/10/25), after the stock has been declining with tariff-induced volatility. Notice the A+ for Valuation.

Luxury Hotel & Resort REIT

Park’s Mission is:

“To be the preeminent lodging REIT, focused on consistently delivering superior, risk-adjusted returns to stockholders through active asset management and a thoughtful growth strategy, while maintaining a strong and flexible balance sheet."

Their strategy is to focus on upper-upscale & luxury full-service, with premier urban and resort destinations, and to build affiliations with dominant global brands. Many of their properties are high-profile names in major cities such as the New York Hilton, Hyatt Boston, Hilton Denver City Center, Royal Palm South Beach Miami, Waldorf Astoria Orlando, etc.

The company calls out its accomplishments and pipeline in the Investor Deck. Starting with completed projects, PK has both renovated and built new buildings. They are currently planning or under construction for about $1 billion worth of real estate, and have future projects in New York and New Orleans.

Q4 and Full Year 2024 Earnings Results

On February 19, 2025, PK announced results for 2024. The quarterly results reflected substantial losses in revenue and income, which were impacted by renovation cost overruns and strike activity. However, the full-year 2024 results showed positive growth.

The financial highlights were as follows:

Q4

  • Comparable RevPAR dropped -1.4% to $179.02 million.
  • Occupancy dropped -1.5 pts to 69.9%.
  • Net Income dropped -61.23% from $188 to $73 million.
  • Adjusted FFO fell -27.3% from $110 to $80 million.

Full Year 2024

  • Total RevPAR up +2.9% to $299.24 million
  • Operating income went up +13.9% to $391 million.
  • Repurchased 8.0 million shares.
  • Declared $1.40 of dividends to stockholders, including a dividend of $.65 during Q4 as a special dividend.
  • Recognized by Newsweek as one of America’s Most Responsible Companies in 2024 and 2025.
  • Closed the 360-room Hilton Oakland Airport, which incurred an EBITDA loss of approximately $4 million.
  • Sold the Hilton La Jolla Torrey Pines, which was owned through JV, for $165 million, collecting $41 million.

Park Has Underperformed the Real Estate Sector

Since going public in 2017, PK has underperformed the real estate sector, The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLRE) on a total return basis (reinvested dividends). This shows that despite the Covid crisis, the stock never quite recovered.

Valuation

Visually, you can see in the chart below that PK’s quarterly dividend is volatile. Nothing was paid out during Covid, the payout rose, then dropped again, leaving investors to worry – what will happen long term? The business model does not produce a predictable income. In my view, the dividend payout is like gambling in Las Vegas, with fun highs, and then no payout at all.

Breaking it out by year is another way to look at it. Again, the frantic up-and*down nature of the distribution does not instill confidence, in my view.

In comparing PK to other hotel and resort REITs, we can see the following conclusions. First, the overall sector does not have an attractive history, given the impact of Covid on travel. For example, in the chart below, all peers show negative 5-year dividend growth rates. In fact, only 3 of 6 showed positive 5-year total returns.

PK stands out amongst the peers with the highest debt-to-equity ratio of 133.3%, which reflects their interest in developing more properties. PK also stands out for their high dividend yield of 13.9%.

Apple Hospitality REIT, Inc. (APLE) is the largest of its peers by market cap. I wonder how many investors bought this REIT thinking it was Apple Inc. (AAPL)? Apple’s 5-year total return is the highest at 51.2% with a substantial dividend of 8.29% and a relatively low debt-to-equity of 48.8%.

Pebblebrook Hotel Trust (PEB) is highly unattractive with a dividend of less than 1%, a 5-year dividend growth rate showing a decline of -48.9% and a negative 5-year total return.

RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) has a decent dividend of 8.1% yield but a declining dividend growth rate and loss over a 5-year total return.

DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH) looks more attractive than some on this list with a 4.4% dividend, a declining dividend growth rate, however positive 5-year total return and one of the lower debt to equity ratios at 75.8%.

Sunstone Hotel Investors, Inc. (SHO) is not that attractive with a 4.18% dividend, a negative 5-year dividend growth rate, and a breakeven 5-year total return.

Outlook

Going into 2025, I’m pessimistic about lodging stocks given the assumption that the tariff war will continue, which will reduce travel in general. Either U.S. citizens going domestic or international, or foreign citizens coming to the U.S., costs will inevitably go up. Therefore, given the cost pressures, PK will not hit its 2025 objectives, in my view.

PK calls for a 2025 outlook where Funds from Operations (AFFO) will end up in the year between a decline of -7.8% and a growth of 6.8%. This type of forecast makes me pause before entrusting my capital to an investment. What they are essentially saying is we could make a profit or a loss, depending on the year. Not inducing confidence.

PK will go ex-dividend approximately June 28, 2025, with an expected dividend of $0.25. The first quarter of the 2025 earnings call will take place on May 5, 2025.

Risks To Rating

The greatest risk to PK comes from its reliance on travel and hospitality. Both will be affected by U.S. policy in the coming months. First, as the tariff war is underway, the threat of higher prices will undermine consumer confidence in traveling. Second, if the U.S. enters a recession given the pressures of higher prices and slower growth, consumers will be less likely to travel domestically and abroad. The cost of real estate development will also be affected, possibly causing project overages and delays.

International travel is likely to be influenced by U.S. policy as well. Leaders from other countries have negative sentiments toward the U.S. discouraging their citizens from traveling to the types of destinations where PK has properties.

Bottom Line

I rate Park Hotels and Resorts a Hold given the risk factors to owning this REIT. The most important factors that influence my opinion include the poor history of navigating crises, inconsistent distribution payout strategy, threats to the travel industry due to the tariff war, and increasing costs. While the dividend yield of 13.9% is appealing at present, an investor needs to look at the long-term total return of owning the security. Since its IPO in 2017, PK has underperformed the real estate sector, and in fact lost money.

PK management points to ongoing projects and the quality of their brands in building their business. However, none of that matters if the cost of travel goes higher and consumers slow down. In fact, some international travelers might be angry at the U.S. and avoid travel based on sentiment. Further, as material costs increase, it is possible that construction projects will be delayed or go over, causing further distress to the stock. For those and other reasons, I recommend investors not only avoid PK but look beyond hotel and resort REITs altogether.

data.title: Netstreit: Defence On The Field Without Sacrificing Yield Exposure
data.description:
The market's topsy-turvy, there's no doubt about that. I mean, the legendary Bill Gross was recently quoted saying: “This is an epic economic and market event similar to 1971, and the end of the gold standard except with immediate negative consequences,” suggesting the tariff-induced market drawdown isn't something to neglect. However, despite fears of a deep market drawdown, we advocate for throughout-the-economic cycle investing. Therefore, we decided to engage with a defensive REIT called NETSTREIT Corp. (NYSE:NTST), which we last covered in May 2024, citing its high-quality portfolio and recession-proof profile.

Unfortunately, given the coverage-to-date surge in higher-risk assets, the REIT hasn't delivered any returns since our analysis. However, considering the current market climate, we think it's time to engage in NTST REIT; here are a few reasons why.

A Defensive Option In A Negative Market Environment

NETSTREIT provides a defensive option within the REIT space and perhaps the broader market environment. Aside from echoing defence via its docile beta coefficient of 0.9 and solid dividend yield, NETSTREIT is fundamentally defensive with a portfolio geared toward essential goods. For example, NTST REIT has notable exposure to CVS Health Corporation (CVS), Dollar General Corporation (DG), Walmart Inc. (WMT), and other staple-esque industries. Moreover, the vehicle is state-diversified, spanning over most of the U.S., nearly 100 long-lease tenants, at a near-perfect occupancy rate.

Here's an expanded view of NTST REIT's defensive exposure, affirming its defensive positioning.

While a tariff war might assert pressure on NTST REIT's tenants on a company level, we don't see much of that pressure flowing through to NTST REIT for three reasons.

  1. The REIT relies on rent and loan originations (minor) as revenue instead of percentage sales, meaning lower tenant revenue bases aren't of direct concern.
  2. Although counter-party risk might increase during a trade war, tenants are locked into long-term leases with obligations to pay. Thus, unless bankruptcy occurs, we'll likely see leases be paid.
  3. A trade war can devalue commercial real estate values through economic deleveraging. However, NTST REIT has a cash and equivalents position north of $14 million and an adjusted leverage ratio of merely 4.5x, suggesting it could become a net buyer during market turbulence, concurrently levelling out unrealised portfolio losses.

Portfolio Performance Review

Financial Results

NETSTREIT's year-over-year revenue grew strongly in fiscal 2024, settling at a figure of nearly $163 million. However, the REIT receded into an accounting loss during the year, largely due to a $29 million+ impairment charge. More specifically, NTST REIT disposed of 35 properties in 2024 and listed 13 as held-for-sale items, resulting in impaired charges. Although NTST REIT recognised a disposition gain, its re-listing of properties as held-for-sale likely repriced its asset base unfavourably.

Although a flimsy economy raises the risk of additional impairments in fiscal 2025, NTST REIT can combat the risk by refraining from dispositions and held-for-sale listings. Moreover, we believe the market might've confused the REIT's accounting profitability with its economic profitability. As illustrated below, NETSTREIT reported more than $148 million in annualised adjusted EBITDA in Q4, suggesting partitioning between the REIT's actual economic performance and accounting results. We anticipate sustained economic stealth throughout fiscal 2025 due to the vehicle's strong asset base and disciplined cost structure (including its capital structure).

Turning to funds from operations. NETSTREIT's funds from operation settled higher in fiscal 2024, growing by 21.63%. However, readers should note that much of the increase was due to impairment losses being dislodged. Nonetheless, we commend the REIT's FFO and AFFO, highlighting its efficient asset-to-liability management.

Operational

Lease Expirations and Walgreens Exposure

A large part of our bullish thesis is centred on NTST REIT's lease ageing schedule. The current macroeconomic environment probably doesn't translate into a fruitful lease renewal environment, let alone a new lease environment. Thus, we were delighted to see that NTST REIT has no leases maturing in 2025 and sub-1% of its current base maturing in 2026.

Acquisitions Vs. Dispositions

NETSTREIT's gross investment activity increased abruptly in Q4, remaining high in general. The vehicle is extending its weighted average lease term with tactical investments, which could grow in time through straight-line rent agreements.

The REIT disposed of numerous properties recently, yet remains a net buyer. Our main reason for juxtaposing acquisition and disposition numbers is to highlight that NTST is buying at higher capitalisation rates than it's selling for. Thus, implying that it's buying low and selling high, lending it the opportunity to achieve tactical profits. Although probably subject to measurement biases, we see this as a feature worth commending.

Capital Structure

A vantage point of NETSTREIT's capital structure shows that it borrows at a weighted average interest rate of 4.53%, similar to some of its peers, including Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (PINE), which borrows at 4.10%. However, NTST REIT uses swap contracts to bring its base down. We think swaps are a clever move, allowing the REIT to lower its cost of capital through manipulating maturity structures.

Furthermore, as previously mentioned, NTST REIT has an adjusted net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 4.5x, which accompanies a total debt/enterprise value ratio of 42.9%. Together, these metrics suggest that NTST REIT has the re-leveraging capacity, which it could utilise if U.S. interest rates fall even further.

Valuation and Dividends

A capitalisation method valuation of NETSTREIT illustrates that its reported net operating income, debt structure, and implied capitalisation ratio (-20 basis points) coalesced a price target of around $15.81, which is in line with the REIT's current market value (April 04, 2025). However, in our view, a lower cap rate can be applied to the valuation due to NTST REIT's elevated AFFO growth rate and flexible capital structure. More specifically, NTST REIT posted an AFFO CAGR of 10.3% between 2021 and 2024; while the rate is expected to decrease to 2.1% in 2025, we still see the REIT as a high growth, flexible capital structure vehicle, and therefore think a cap rate of 6.5% can be justified, which would lead its fair value above $19 per share.

Furthermore, NTST REIT's dividend profile seems robust with the REIT yielding above 5% with a moderate payout ratio of 66% and four years of consecutive growth. In addition, the REIT's dividend profile stacks up well against its peers. For example, NTST REIT's yield outpaces those of Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) and Essential Properties Realty Trust, Inc. (EPRT), while aligning with those of Realty Income Corporation (O), NNN REIT, Inc. (NNN), and Four Corners Property Trust, Inc. (FCPT). Moreover, recall that we mentioned NTST REIT's defensive profile, which, we think, could sustain its dividend throughout messy economic periods.

Risks

It goes without saying that the current trade war has elevated market risk. As an illustration, the Nasdaq slipped into a bear market on Friday, shortly after China retaliated with tariffs of their own. A sustained trade war could buckle the global financial markets holistically, including defensive REITs such as NTST REIT.

An additional risk is NTST REIT's ongoing impairments. A drawdown in commercial property values in 2025 might result in additional accounting losses, contemporaneously spooking investors.

Concluding Thoughts

We think NETSTREIT provides a solid defensive REIT opportunity. Despite market headwinds, the REIT doesn't have notable short-term lease expirations. Moreover, NTST possesses long-term agreements with staple industry tenants.

Furthermore, we think NTST's dividend yield is highly alluring, and while its valuation is speculative, we don't see the REIT as grossly overvalued.

Consensus: 'Buy'.

data.title: Leggett & Platt: Down And Nearly Out
data.description:
Leggett & Platt (NYSE:LEG), once a reliable dividend growth company with a rock solid reputation as a stalwart of American manufacturing, has come up against its greatest test in history as a perfect storm of unfavorable business trends and regulatory uncertainty threatens its ability to remain profitable. As recently as early 2024, Leggett seemed as if it would weather the economic storm of inflation and reduced discretionary spending while continuing to raise dividends as a member of the vaunted Dividend Kings, companies that had raised their dividend annually for 50 or more years consecutively. However, investor confidence was shaken to the core when in April 2024, the company slashed its dividend from a whopping $0.46 per share to a measly $0.05 per share as mounting debt and stiff competition from overseas manufacturers pressured margins to the breaking point.

With a downfall catalyzed by misguided acquisitions, especially into the specialty foam business via Elite Comfort Solutions in 2018, Leggett failed to follow market trends in mattress manufacturing and sales. Specifically, consumers have increasingly shifted towards buying lower-end, basic mattresses at a more frequent cadence as opposed to purchasing luxury brands or products such as specialty foam mattresses. As such, the ECS purchase has proved to be a costly mistake, ballooning long-term debt from $1.16B in 2018 to $2.06B in 2019 with little to show for it other than increased interest payments. As a result, in August 2023, Fitch downgraded the company’s credit rating from BBB to BBB-, putting its bonds on the precipice of being no longer investment grade. While restructuring efforts are now underway to emphasize the most profitable portions of Leggett’s operations, it feels as if there is little hope for recovery, especially with a recent divestiture of the Aerospace Products Group that provided essential product diversification and drove slight revenue growth to help offset mattress losses in Q4 2024.

Can They Bounce Back?

Leggett & Platt was left with little choice in early 2024 but to initiate a wind down of numerous manufacturing facilities via a comprehensive restructuring effort, as diminished sales volume enterprise-wide left a huge footprint of their operations operating at far less than optimal capacity. To remedy this, to date Leggett has closed 14 bedding facilities, one home furniture facility, and one flooring products facility, realizing $22 million in additional earnings via reductions in overhead and administrative costs. However, despite these efforts, sales are guided to continue their downward slide in 2025 by between 2% and 9%. As was demonstrated during the Biden administration, inflation hits Leggett's product portfolio especially hard, as bedding products have been shown to be very price sensitive, with customers shying away from premium bedding or from bedding altogether when increased costs of materials yield higher prices. As such, it is difficult to see a silver lining on the horizon, with restructuring earnings improvements seeming to be a drop in the bucket compared to continued market share losses that may or may not return.

Valuation

Leggett is a company in crisis, and it is priced accordingly, with a price/earnings multiple of ~6.5x and a price/sales multiple of ~0.25. Unfortunately, the company seems to be attempting to squeeze as much margin as possible from an increasingly smaller slice of a highly constrained market, which would be difficult to achieve under the best of circumstances. As such, it is hard not to come to the conclusion that this is a value trap, a melting ice cube with little hope of recovering to past share prices that were in the $30-$40 range only a few years ago.

Risks

The best hope for upside risk in Leggett's case is a dovish Fed, which would help to cushion the blow of inflated debt levels by lowering the cost of refinancing and increasing consumer sentiment, hopefully driving additional housing starts and consequently increased mattress demand. Ironically, as a vertically integrated company with most of its manufacturing based in the United States, Leggett & Platt may also be a beneficiary of protectionist tariff policies implemented by the Trump administration, especially the higher duties applied to Chinese products that directly compete with Leggett's more expensive and higher-end bedding products. However, there is tremendous uncertainty as to the staying power of any tariff policy implemented by the Trump administration, so the long-term effects are difficult to gauge at best.

Conclusion

Overall, Leggett & Platt is extremely difficult to recommend as an investment, despite the impression of a company seeing its proverbial "blood in the streets" moment. I believe that the recent bets Leggett made, especially investing in specialty foam, handicapped the company's success to such an extent that it may not ultimately recover. The most telling sign of this is its divestiture of the Aerospace Products Group for $285 million, an otherwise successful and growing segment. In conclusion, I rate Leggett as a SELL, as it should be avoided as an investment until secular sales trends improve.

data.title: MGM Resorts: Balancing Las Vegas Strip Weakness, BetMGM Improvements
data.description:
MGM Resorts International (NYSE:MGM) is a very diversified gaming company. The company operates 16 casinos within the U.S. at the end of 2024, including Aria, the Bellagio, MGM Grand, and other Las Vegas strip casinos as well as regional casinos. MGM also owns around 56% of two Macau casinos in China and is currently developing a 50%-owned casino in Osaka, Japan through a joint venture. The casino in Japan is planned to open in 2030. The company is also eyeing New York with a bid for the MGM Empire City, among other investments. Additionally, MGM operates online casinos with a 50% stake in BetMGM and through LeoVegas.

The company’s overall stable performance has caused MGM’s stock to appreciate at quite a slow 3.1% CAGR in the past decade. Although MGM has very good cash flow, recent quarters have shown some earnings weakness in the Las Vegas strip, causing MGM to depreciate. MGM doesn’t pay a dividend, instead choosing to spend cash flow on extensive share buybacks.

MGM’s Las Vegas Performance Has Been Slow Recently

Overall, excluding pandemic-time earnings, MGM’s long-term financial performance has been relatively stable as the company’s casinos reach a massive number of customers. The company has grown revenues at a 2002-2024 CAGR of 7.5% through significant casino investments. MGM’s profitability has also been fairly good. The company currently has a fair, stable operating margin of 9.6% after a number of significant property sale-and-leasebacks in previous years. The return on equity stands at a good 21.8% level.

While MGM’s past operational performance has been stable over the long term, the company’s more recent performance has also shown weakness. Most recently, in MGM’s Q4, revenues even declined -1% to $4.35 billion due to weak room bookings. MGM faced challenging comparison figures in Las Vegas casinos due to strong Q4 2023 Formula 1 traffic, causing the largest segment’s sales to drop -6% to $2.22 billion through weaker room and casino revenues. Adjusted EBITDAR for the Las Vegas segment declined -11% to $0.77 billion through negative operating leverage. According to Nevada Gaming Control Board, Las Vegas strip’s industry-wide gaming revenues declined -3.2% year-on-year in Q4, making MGM’s performance in the segment slightly worse than an already slow industry performance.

At the same time, MGM’s regional casinos still showed a very strong performance in Q4, and MGM China (OTCPK:MCHVF) still reported solid revenue growth and fair earnings. MGM also exited 2024 with strong momentum in December and January in Las Vegas as well as regional casinos, and industry-wide gaming revenues for the Las Vegas strip have grown 0.8% in the December-February period, showing better prospects for MGM after a relatively weak Q4. I believe that MGM’s earnings should be relatively stable going forward, albeit the Las Vegas weakness could still cause some pressure on 2025 growth. MGM’s Macau operations have continued to gain market share, improving the outlook.

BetMGM Is Projected to Become Profitable in 2025

MGM’s digital BetMGM platform is still unprofitable, generating -$22 million in adjusted EBITDA losses in Q4, as the company is facing tough competition from DraftKings (DKNG) and FanDuel (FLUT). Yet, the prospects for MGM’s digital earnings also seem to be gradually improving.

After continuing to lose market share in the 2021-2023 period, BetMGM has stabilized its iGaming market share performance in 2024, now holding a 20.5% market share in US iGaming as of February. MGM has increased marketing expenses for the segment to drive improved revenues, already clearly showing results – in Q4, MGM’s digital operations generated $140 million in revenues, up 15% year-on-year. BetMGM has also stabilized its market position in sports betting with a 7.7% market share in February, but the market share in sports betting remains low compared to DraftKings and FanDuel. Ultimately, MGM now targets digital operations to generate $1000 million in revenues in the mid-term, signaling ambition for much further growth.

Notably, after still generating a -$244 million EBITDA loss in 2024, BetMGM is already projected to become profitable in 2025. After MGM’s BetMGM has been a drag on earnings for quite long, the segment is at least reaching profitability and could even start to contribute positively towards earnings as BetMGM’s market position has stabilized in a well-growing market.

Valuation

I built a discounted cash flow (DCF) model for MGM’s stock to estimate a fair value. After still stagnant revenue expectations for 2025, I ultimately estimate MGM’s revenue growth to stabilize at an annual rate of 2%, signaling slow growth in physical casinos and slightly better growth from digital operations. For 2030, I estimate total revenue growth of 8% due to the planned casino opening in Japan. I estimate the EBIT margin to reach 10% through operating leverage in MGM’s digital operations.

MGM’s cash flow should remain quite healthy, even though the company’s growth investments do weigh on the cash flow outlook. I have subtracted minority interest regarding MGM’s Chinese operations and BetMGM from the estimated free cash flow.

The estimates put MGM’s fair value at $28.57, near the stock price at the time of writing – for now, I estimate the stock to be fairly valued. MGM could potentially generate better cash flow than I anticipate if digital earnings ramp up very well, but for now, I believe that the DCF model’s estimates make a fair base scenario.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 9.98%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.83% interest rate, annualized from Q4 interest expenses and debt. I estimate MGM to continue to leverage debt quite substantially, with a 60% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.31% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. Seeking Alpha estimates MGM’s beta at 1.59. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 12.89% and the WACC at 9.98%.

Takeaway

MGM has had quite stable operations, more recently disrupted by weakness in the Las Vegas strip. Regardless, through a strong regional performance and good market share growth in China, MGM’s overall earnings outlook remains stable. The company’s BetMGM digital platform has stabilized market share during 2024 with heavy marketing investments, and now looks to turn profitable in 2025 through revenue growth. The stock is priced fairly, and as such, I initiate MGM Resorts at a Hold rating.
