data.title: Delta Air Lines Has Tools Necessary To Weather Macro Storm, But It Remains Unattractive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

My last article on Delta Air Lines (NYSE:DAL), published in January 2025, was a preview of its Q4 earnings numbers. In the article, I explored the key factors that investors need to watch out for in the company’s Q4 report, with a special focus on the impact of the holiday season as well as the CrowdStrike lawsuit. I had a Hold rating on the stock.

Since the article was published, the stock has done a complete round trip, hitting all-time highs at one point before crashing back to Earth. All in all, the stock declined nearly 32% during this period, significantly underperforming the S&P 500, which declined 11.1% during the same period.

A lot has happened since then, especially on the macroeconomic front. Today, DAL operates in an environment riddled with so much uncertainty that the airline even had to slash its Q1 guidance a month before the actual report. The company reported its Q1 numbers yesterday, and in this article, I analyze the report as well as examine the company’s plans to navigate the cloudy, turbulent, macroenvironment.

First Quarter Highlights

DAL had a relatively strong start to the year, with a beat on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. Q1 revenues came in at $14.04 billion, up 2.1% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $582.9 million. Non-GAAP adjusted EPS came in at $0.46, also up 2% y/y and beating analyst estimates by $0.07. Total Revenue per Available Seat Mile (TRASM), however, was down 1% y/y and while nonfuel unit costs (CASM-ex) were up 2.6% y/y, they were better than what management had guided for and were down 1% sequentially. Operating margins were marginally lower, coming in at 4.6%, compared to 5.1% during the same period last year. Free cash flows came in at $1.28 billion, and while this was down 7% y/y, the figure was after accounting for $1.4 billion that was paid out in profit sharing and after investing $1.2 billion back into the business.

For the second quarter, the company expects revenue growth to be between -2% and +2%. Adjusted EPS is expected to come in between $1.70 and $2.30, with the mid-point value of $2.00 falling below consensus estimates of $2.29. Operating margins are expected to significantly improve, expected to come in anywhere between 11% and 14%.

Due to the significant economic uncertainty and the unpredictability surrounding economic growth, brought about by the Trump Administration’s tariff policies, which are becoming more and more unpredictable with each passing day, DAL, rightfully in my opinion, announced that it was pulling its full-year guidance. However, it did give indications on what to expect for the rest of the year. For instance, the company plans to reduce capacity growth in the second half of the year to flat y/y in order to align supply with demand. Management also reiterated their confidence in delivering a profitable year, generating strong free cash flow, and are also expecting to repay at least $3 billion of their outstanding debt in FY25. After the earnings release, shares finished the session up nearly 23.5%, although this was primarily driven by President Trump’s announcement of a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs on all countries except China.

DAL's Customer Base and Diversified Revenue Streams Should Help It to Weather Any Slowdown

As I mentioned earlier, DAL’s decision to pull its full-year guidance makes perfect sense in my opinion. The airline already had to slash its Q1 guidance in March, about a month before it reported its Q1 numbers, on account of domestic softness. And it was a dramatic cut, with revenue growth slashed from 7-9% to 3-4%, and EPS guidance slashed from a midpoint of $0.85 to a midpoint of $0.45, a figure it marginally beat. More important than the magnitude of the cut, it was the timing of the cut that took me by surprise. Such has been the degree of uncertainty in the macroeconomic backdrop in recent times that there is no way, in my opinion, any full-year guidance that the company offered along with its Q1 numbers would have stood with each passing quarter. So, it was sensible for the company to pull its guidance rather than having to keep dramatically altering it with each passing quarter, which would have only made investors more jittery.

Even without the full-year outlook, however, there still was a lot of evidence, based on management commentary and the Q1 results, which suggests that DAL has the necessary tools to withstand the heavy turbulence that is expected in the months ahead. The airline has a well-diversified revenue stream that should shield it, to an extent, from any economic slowdown in the U.S. For instance, in Q1, both premium and loyalty revenues were up around 7% y/y and remuneration from American Express grew 13% y/y to $2 billion. Moreover, cargo revenues also increased 17% y/y. All of these were more than enough to offset the softness seen in the domestic main cabins and the choppiness seen in corporate travel, resulting in an overall revenue growth. At the same time, the company has already announced that it would keep second-half capacity growth flat y/y, on account of weakness seen in the domestic main cabin seats. Moreover, management also announced that they would be “aggressively managing” their cost base to “reflect the lower level of flying.”

To me, the biggest takeaway from the aforementioned Q1 developments is the divergence seen in the demand for premium and main cabin. During any period of economic uncertainty, one would expect a trade-down from premium to lower levels. In DAL’s case, this hasn’t transpired yet, which to me is a huge positive, as it implies that the higher revenues gained from premium can more than offset any declines seen in the Main Cabin. Management, thankfully, was able to shed some light during the earnings call on this paradox. More specifically, DAL’s customer base predominantly consists of higher-income consumers, who earn more than $100,000 annually. These consumers tend to be less affected by any economic downturn, and they tend to prioritize premiumisation, which explains the growing divergence between premium bookings and Main Cabin bookings. This also explains the double-digit growth seen in the remuneration from American Express. As long as this section of the U.S. consumer base dominates DAL’s customer base, the airline should be able to maximize its premium and AmEx revenue levers, which should help to offset any weakness seen in the Main Cabin.

Finally, there is international travel, which has surprisingly held up even during these uncertain times, with the exception of Canada. Transatlantic travel, in particular, continues to see strong demand, with management announcing that international travel demand remains very strong throughout the summer, and “all the way out to September, October.” Once again, the composition of the customer base explains this confounding development. In addition to predominantly belonging to the higher-income category, management also announced that the average age of a Delta One customer is in the 60s, which implies that there is a kind of “wealth effect,” whereby individuals in this cohort tend to be wealthier than any other cohort, that’s driving international travel. It should also, therefore, come as no surprise that even within the international travel category, premiumisation continues to drive demand, which further lends support to my thesis on why DAL has the necessary tools to withstand the heavy economic turbulence that lies ahead.

DAL is by no means fully immune from any potential economic downturn. No airline company would be. The plans to reduce capacity in the second half of the year and the continued weakness seen in domestic travel are evidence of this. The airline also announced that they are not taking any deliveries that have tariffs on them, which could have a negative impact on the company’s fleet composition going forward. Having said that, DAL, in my opinion, remains well-positioned to minimize the negative impact of any potential economic downturn, thanks to its diversified revenue streams. In most of my articles on DAL, I have always mentioned how having a diversified revenue stream has helped DAL to register strong growth. Those were the good times. Even during times of uncertainty, it is this same catalyst, with a little help from the higher-income customer base, that has come to the airline’s rescue.

Valuation

Forward P/E Approach

Price Target

$39.00

Projected Forward P/E Multiple

6.4x

Projected FY25 EPS

$5.81

Forward PEG Ratio

1.19

FY25 Earnings Growth

5.4%

Projected FY25 EPS

$6.12

Source: DAL’s Q1 FY25 Earnings Release, LSEG Workspace (formerly Refinitiv), Seeking Alpha, and Author’s Calculations

The company, as mentioned earlier, has pulled its full-year guidance. The guidance that it gave, at the time it released its Q4 numbers, was a projected FY25 EPS of more than $7.35, implying an EPS growth of 10%. However, during that time, management had guided for a projected Q1 EPS of between $0.70 and $1.00, well above its actual Q1 EPS of $0.46 (this was, however, marginally above the revised Q1 EPS guidance issued last month). The actual EPS is about 54% lower than the original projection, which implies that the actual FY25 EPS would be well below $7.35.

For Q2, the company expects EPS to come in between $1.70 and $2.30. Even the high-end of the company’s guidance would imply a y/y decline of about 3%. If one assumes the midpoint of the Q2 guide, $2.00, this would imply that total EPS for the first half of FY25 would be $2.46, a decline of 12.5% compared to the same period last year.

According to LSEG Data, the company’s average long-term EPS growth rate stands at 6.8%, and at this growth rate, the FY25 EPS should come in at $6.58, which looks extremely unlikely despite the growth seen in premium and international because of the planned capacity reduction as well as the weakness seen in the domestic Main Cabin. The total EPS for the second half of FY24 was $3.35 ($1.50 in Q3 and $1.85 in Q4). Assuming as a result of cost reduction and the continued growth seen in premium and international, the company matches this EPS figure, then the total EPS for FY25 is projected to come in at $5.81, which translates to a decline of 5.7% y/y. This, to me, is a reasonable estimate, and is slightly above the average street estimate of $5.66.

DAL, according to LSEG Data (formerly Refinitiv), currently trades at a forward P/E of 7.19x, higher than its peers such as American Airlines (AAL) (6.02x) and United Airlines (UAL) (6.13x), but significantly lower than Southwest Airlines (LUV) (14.9x). The airline, however, is currently trading in line with its historical 5-year forward P/E multiple. However, given the projected decline in FY25 EPS growth, I am of the opinion that it should be trading at a lower multiple. As such, I have assumed a forward P/E of 6.4x, which is its 2-year forward P/E multiple.

According to Seeking Alpha, DAL currently trades at a forward PEG ratio of 1.19, lower than the sector median of 1.52. A forward P/E of 6.4x and a forward PEG ratio of 1.19 implies an EPS growth of 5.4%, which is a reasonable growth in my opinion for FY26 as the uncertainty surrounding tariffs should become a lot clearer in the next twelve months. This is the EPS growth that I have assumed for my calculations, which translates to a projected FY26 EPS of $6.12.

A forward P/E of 6.4x and a projected FY26 EPS of $6.12 implies a price target of $39, which is around the levels that it’s currently trading at. When you combine this with a short-term momentum grade of D and an overall momentum grade of C-, according to Seeking Alpha’s Momentum scale, I wouldn’t be buying the shares at current levels. As such, I am maintaining my HOLD rating on the stock.

Concluding Thoughts

DAL had a relatively strong start to the year, beating on both the top- and bottom-line estimates. However, due to the economic uncertainty brought about by the prospects of a global trade war, management pulled its full-year guidance. The second quarter EPS guide also fell short of consensus estimates.

There is no doubt that DAL faces significant turbulence ahead, especially with weakness set to persist in the domestic Main cabin category. However, in my opinion, the combination of diversified revenue streams such as premium and remuneration from American Express, the continued demand seen in international travel, and a significant proportion of the customer base made up of higher-income consumers should help the airline to weather the economic uncertainty. The planned capacity reduction should also help.

From a valuation perspective, DAL remains fairly valued at current levels, especially since I don’t see a way by which, the company can generate an EPS growth in FY25. As such, I would wait for any meaningful pullback before considering buying the stock. It also doesn’t help that the momentum in the stock has been weak.

DAL continues to remain a high-quality name and has all the tools required to emerge out of this turbulent macro backdrop with minimal damage. However, despite the catalysts mentioned in this article, and despite the round-trip that the stock has taken from its all-time highs, I continue to remain hesitant when it comes to boarding the stock.
